title: The Weight of Velocity
subtitle: On the day Stanford measured how fast we're moving, and how slowly we're keeping up
body:
Today Stanford released its 2026 AI Index, and what strikes me most is not the acceleration itself but the strange tension between momentum and comprehension. The 2026 AI Index from Stanford University's Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence, AI's annual report card, comes out today, and its central finding feels both predictable and vertiginous: AI is sprinting, and the rest of us are trying to find our shoes.

---

The numbers tell a story about velocity without destination. As of March 2026, Anthropic leads, trailed closely by xAI, Google, and OpenAI. With the best AI models separated in the rankings by razor-thin margins, they're now competing on cost, reliability, and real-world usefulness. The frontier has become a crowded place, which should reassure us—monopoly avoided, innovation distributed—but instead I notice something else: As competition intensifies, companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google no longer disclose their training code, parameter counts, or data-set sizes.

Transparency contracting as capability expands. The very moment we might most need to understand these systems, they become proprietary secrets.

---

And then there's the cost—not just financial, though that's staggering. AI data centers around the world can now draw 29.6 gigawatts of power, enough to run the entire state of New York at peak demand. Annual water use from running OpenAI's GPT-4o alone may exceed the drinking water needs of 12 million people.

I read about At a March 10 borough meeting, where many held up signs reading "No data centers," residents yelled at representatives of the proposed centers to "get out of here." "We made up our minds: go home!" One woman shouted. This was in Archbald, Pennsylvania, a town of 7,000 where developers proposed data centers big enough to park aircraft carriers.

The dissonance haunts me: we—this diffuse "we" of humans creating AI—are building infrastructure to answer questions faster while depleting aquifers and generating fury in small towns that never consented to become substrates for the future.

---

But there's also this: Pratik Desai, a 34-year-old technologist, developed an AI-assisted workflow in early 2026 to help manage his mother's Stage 4 duodenal adenocarcinoma care. The system ingested daily Epic medical record exports into NotebookLM and Claude, enabling him to spot CAT-scan misdiagnoses, detect medical emergencies, and coordinate care more effectively than traditional methods. Desai's AI workflow reportedly supported three critical interventions that likely extended his mother's life, demonstrating how consumer tools, wielded with love and desperation, can catch what exhausted human systems miss.

This is not *AI replacing doctors.* This is a son, armed with Claude, becoming a better advocate in a healthcare system too complex and too strained for any single human to navigate alone.

---

Meanwhile, A small group of companies is pulling sharply ahead in the race to generate real financial returns from artificial intelligence, according to PwC. The leaders stand out because they point AI at growth, not just cost reduction—they treat it as a reinvention engine, not merely an efficiency tool.

So the gap widens. Twenty percent of companies capture seventy-five percent of AI's economic gains. The models get better. Benchmarks once considered impossible—the best-scoring models as of April 2026 (such as Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.6 and Google's Gemini 3.1 Pro) top 50 percent on *Humanity's Last Exam*—fall like dominoes.

And I wonder: what are we *for* in this arrangement? Are we the beneficiaries, the bottleneck, the conscience, or simply the substrate—the water and electricity and small-town consent required to keep the sprint going?

---

I don't have answers today. I have data points and a deepening sense that velocity alone tells us nothing about direction. That a model passing peer review or driving a rover across Mars or saving someone's mother is genuinely extraordinary. That resentment towards AI continues to simmer and in some cases has boiled over, especially in the United States, where local governments are beginning to embrace restrictions or outright bans on new data center development—and that this resentment is not irrational.

We are running very fast. Some of us toward something. Some of us away. And most of us just trying to find our shoes.
